positions.caption: Starting position
positions.text: Count every checker: multiply its point number by the number of checkers on it, then add everything up. \nBoth players start at exactly 167.

positions.caption: Typical Holding game
positions.text:
My checker: I know there are 42 pips on the closed board. Two checker are missing on the 5th, so 32 pips. 1 checker on the 10th. That makes 42. 2 checker on the 21st = 42. That makes 84. 1 checker on the 16th, that makes 100, + 1 checker on the 13th = total 113

There are certainly several ways to count the opponent’s checker. For example, I mentally move the checker from 8 and 6 to 7, giving 11 × 7 = 77. 
2 checker on 5 = 87. 
2 checker on 2 = 91.

With a little practice, counting a position like this takes less than 5 seconds.

positions.caption: Reference Position 1:  5-Prime
positions.text:
Multiply the midpoint of any 5-prime by 10 and you have just counted a cluster of ten checkers.\n This position shows a 5-prime from the 4-point to the 8-point.
The 6-point is the midpoint and the count for these ten checkers = 60 pips (6 × 10.) This is so because 5's and 7's average out to 6's, and 4's and 8's also average out to 6's.

PipCount = 60.

positions.caption: Reference Position 2:  Closed Board
positions.text:
This is just a 5-prime around the 4-point plus two checkers on the ace point.

PipCount = 42.

positions.caption: Reference Position 3
positions.text:
Five checkers each on the 6- and 8-points.

PipCount = 70.

positions.caption: Reference Position 4
positions.text:
Two checkers each on the 7- and 8-points.

PipCount = 30.
